05 Filling in your Team Directory - YouTube.mp4
This step is actually pretty simple. All you're doing is filling in the name, job title, and other basic details for each member of your different teams into the high output team canvas. Now, if your team is small, you might be tempted to skip this step. But don't do that, okay? Just start with the first core functional team and ask, 'Who currently serves on this team?' Now, the leader of this team should be listed. First, it doesn't matter if they're a manager, director, vice president, C-level, or head of. Just write the name of the person who is primarily in charge of this team, and then list everyone else who is on that team below the team leader. But I know what you're thinking. What if a team doesn't have a leader?
Well, this happens a lot, especially in smaller organizations. And the solution is. Pretty simple. If a team is currently leaderless, then good news. You are that team's leader. That's right. Every team must have a leader. management role is vacant, the next leader up in the chain of command needs to fill it until a suitable replacement is found. Similarly, you may also find that one person performs multiple jobs across multiple teams. So what do you do in those cases? Again, the solution is simple. Acknowledge reality by adding their name to each and every team that they're on. For example, if your marketing person also answers customer support tickets, then they aren't just a marketing person. The reality is, they actually have two different jobs, and in this case, marketing and customer support, and they may even be reporting into two different managers.
Now, if this happens, don't panic and definitely don't go into denial. The goal of this exercise isn't to give you or anyone else more work. The goal is simply to clarify the work that is already being done and who is already doing it, so we can identify both gaps and areas of overwhelm in the organization. Now, if you find yourself saying, 'Okay, literally everybody on this team is also on this team,' then it may make sense just to collapse those two teams into one. But again, don't overthink it. The goal of this exercise is not to paint a picture of the ideal organizational structure. The goal is to take a snapshot of what is today. The work that is currently being done and who is currently doing That works. Eventually, you'll leverage this information to build a hiring plan and organizational chart, but that can't happen until everyone on your team is crystal clear on the tasks that matter most and who is uniquely accountable to those tasks. And fortunately, that's what the next step is all about.
